name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (١٩٠)
```

```english
Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you, but do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like transgressors. (190)
```

```arabic
وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَخْرِجُوهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ أَخْرَجُوكُمْ ۚ وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَشَدُّ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ حَتَّىٰ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِيهِ ۖ فَإِن قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٩١)
```

```english
And kill them wherever you confront them, and expel them from where they expelled you, for persecution is graver than killing. But do not fight them near the Holy Mosque unless they fight you therein; but if they fight you, kill them; such is the requital of the faithless. (191)
```

```arabic
فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٩٢)
```

```english
But if they desist, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (192)
```

```arabic
وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ لِلَّهِ ۖ فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٩٣)
```

```english
Fight them until persecution is no more, and religion becomes [exclusively] for Allah. Then if they desist, there shall be no reprisal except against the wrongdoers. (193)
```

```arabic
ٱلشَّهْرُ ٱلْحَرَامُ بِٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَٱلْحُرُمَـٰتُ قِصَاصٌ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱعْتَدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٩٤)
```

```english
A sacred month for a sacred month, and all sanctities require retribution. So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah, and know that Allah is with the Godwary. (194)
```

```arabic
وَأَنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تُلْقُوا۟ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى ٱلتَّهْلُكَةِ ۛ وَأَحْسِنُوٓا۟ ۛ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٩٥)
```

```english
Spend in the way of Allah, and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction; and be virtuous. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous. (195)
```

## (Commentary)

The context of these noble verses indicates that they were revealed all at once. The discourse within them is directed toward a single purpose: the initial legislation of fighting against the polytheists of Mecca. They address the matter of expelling them from where they expelled the believers, as well as the issues of persecution (الفتنة), retaliation (القصاص), and the prohibition of fighting them near the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) unless they initiate combat there. All these matters are tied to the polytheists of Mecca. Furthermore, the Exalted restricted the fighting to those who initiate it in His saying: "And fight in the way of Allah those who fight you." This does not imply a condition—meaning "fight them *if* they fight you"—as that is obvious; nor is it a restrictive qualification. Some have suggested it means "fight the men and not the women and children who do not fight you," but this is incorrect. There is no sense in "fighting" someone incapable of combat such that one would be prohibited from "fighting" them; rather, the correct expression would be a prohibition against "killing" them, not "fighting" them.

Rather, it is apparent that the verb—meaning "those who fight you" (يقاتلونكم)—denotes the present state and serves as a descriptive attribute for identification. It refers to those whose current state is one of combat against the believers, namely the polytheists of Mecca.

Thus, the course of these verses follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: **أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِن دِيَارِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّا أَن يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ** — "Permission [to fight] has been given to those who are being fought, because they have been wronged. Indeed Allah is capable of helping them—those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah.’" (22:39-40). This is an initial permission for fighting against the combatant polytheists without condition.

Moreover, all five verses are concerned with explaining a single ruling with its boundaries, aspects, and implications. His saying, the Exalted: "And fight in the way of Allah," establishes the core ruling. His saying: "and do not transgress," defines its limits in terms of order. His saying: "And kill them [wherever you confront them]," defines it in terms of severity. His saying: "and do not fight them at the Sacred Mosque," defines it in terms of place. His saying: "And fight them until there is no more persecution," defines it in terms of duration and time. His saying: "The sacred month [for the sacred month]," explains that this ruling is a legislation for retaliation (القصاص) in combat and killing, and a reciprocal treatment of them. His saying: "And spend," makes mandatory its financial prerequisite, which is spending for equipment and preparation. Thus, it is likely that the entirety of the five verses was revealed for a single occasion without some abrogating (الناسخ) others, as some have conjectured, nor were they revealed for disparate matters as others have mentioned. Rather, their purpose is one: the legislation of fighting against the polytheists of Mecca who were fighting the believers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وقاتلوا في سبيل الله الذين يقاتلونكم (And fight in the way of Allah those who fight you). Fighting (القتال) is a man's attempt to kill one who is attempting to kill him. Its being "in the way of Allah" is because the purpose behind it is the establishment of the religion (الدين) and the exaltation of the word of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. Thus, it is an act of worship (العبادة) intended for the sake of Allah the Exalted, not for seizing people's wealth or violating their honor. In Islam, it is a defense (دفاع) by which the legitimate rights of humanity are preserved according to the sound primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — as we shall explain. Defense is inherently limited, and transgression (التعدي) is a departure from that limit; therefore, He followed it with His saying: "and do not transgress; indeed Allah does not love the transgressors."

**His saying, the Exalted:** ولا تعتدوا (and do not transgress). Transgression (الاعتداء) is the departure from a limit. It is said "he crossed" (عدا) and "he transgressed" (اعتدى) when he exceeded his bound. The prohibition of transgression is absolute, intended to cover everything that qualifies as transgression, such as fighting before inviting to the truth, initiating combat, killing women and children, not ceasing when the enemy does, and other matters explained by the Prophetic Sunnah (السنة النبوية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** واقتلوهم حيث ثقفتموهم (And kill them wherever you confront them) until His saying: [persecution is worse] than killing. It is said "he found" (ثقف ثقافة) meaning he encountered or perceived. Thus, the meaning of the verse is the same as His saying: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ** — "then kill the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:5). Persecution (الفتنة) — *the act of testing or trial* — is that by which the state of a thing is tested. Therefore, it is applied to the trial and tribulation itself, and to what often accompanies it, such as hardship and torment, as well as what follows it, such as misguidance and polytheism (الشرك). It has been used in the Noble Quran in all these senses. What is intended in the verse is polytheism against Allah and His Messenger through coercion and torment, as the polytheists in Mecca used to do to the believers both before and after the migration of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.

The meaning is: be as severe as possible against the polytheists in Mecca by killing them wherever they are found, until it leads to their departure from their homes and their exile from their land, just as they did that to you. And what they did was worse, for that was persecution (فتنة) on their part, and persecution is worse than killing. This is because in killing there is the cessation of the life of this world, while in persecution there is the cessation of both lives and the destruction of both abodes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ولا تقاتلوهم عند المسجد الحرام حتى يقاتلوكم فيه (and do not fight them at the Sacred Mosque unless they fight you therein). This contains a prohibition against fighting near the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) to preserve its sanctity so long as they preserve it. The pronoun in His saying "therein" (فيه) refers to the place indicated by His saying "at the Mosque."

**His saying, the Exalted:** فإِن انتهوا فإِن الله غفور رحيم (But if they relinquish, then Allah is indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful). Relinquishing (الانتهاء) is to refrain and desist. What is intended here is desisting from fighting near the Sacred Mosque specifically, not desisting from all fighting by obeying the religion and accepting Islam; for the latter is what is intended by His second saying: "then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility." As for this relinquishing, it is a condition referring to the sentence closest to it, which is His saying: "and do not fight them at the Sacred Mosque." Accordingly, each of the two sentences—namely His saying: "But if they relinquish, then Allah..." and His saying: "then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility"—is a condition for the speech connected to it, without repetition.

In His saying: "then Allah is indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful," the cause is placed in the position of the effect to provide the rationale for the ruling. The meaning is: if they desist, then Allah is All-forgiving and All-merciful [toward them].

**His saying, the Exalted:** وقاتلوهم حتى لا تكون فتنه ويكون الدين لله (Fight them until there is no more persecution, and religion belongs [entirely] to Allah). This defines the duration of the fighting, as previously mentioned. Persecution (الفتنة) in the language of these verses is polytheism (الشرك) through the taking of idols, as the polytheists in Mecca used to do and coerce others toward. This is indicated by His saying: "and religion belongs to Allah." The verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وإِن تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ مُولَاكُمْ نِعْمَ الْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ** — "Fight them until there is no [more] persecution, and religion becomes [exclusively] for Allah. But if they desist, then Allah is indeed watchful of what they do." (8:39). The verse contains an indication of the obligation of invitation (الدعوة) before fighting; if it is accepted, there is no fighting, and if it is rejected, then there is no guardianship except for Allah, and He is the best Guardian and the best Helper, helping His believing servants. It is well known that fighting is only so that religion belongs to Allah, and there is no sense in fighting of this nature and goal except after an invitation to the true religion—the religion that is established upon monotheism (التوحيد).

It appears from what we have mentioned that this verse is not abrogated by His saying, the Exalted: **قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَلَا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ** — "Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, being wretched." (9:29), based on the premise that their religion is for Allah, the Glorified. This is because the verse—meaning His saying: "And fight them until there is no more persecution"—is specific to the polytheists and does not include the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). Thus, the meaning of "religion belonging to Allah" is that idols are not worshipped and monotheism is affirmed. The People of the Book affirm monotheism, even if that is technically disbelief (كفر) in Allah according to the reality, as He said: "they do not have faith in Allah nor in the Last Day... nor practise the true religion." However, Islam is content with them merely affirming monotheism, and only commanded fighting them until they pay the tribute (الجزية) to exalt the word of truth over theirs and to make Islam manifest over all religion.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فإِن انتهوا فلا عدوان إِلا على الظالمين (then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility except against the wrongdoers). That is, if they desist from persecution and believe in what you believe in, then do not fight them, for there is no hostility except against the wrongdoers (الظالمين). This is another instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect, as seen previously in His saying: "But if they relinquish, then Allah is indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful." The verse is like His saying, the Exalted: **فإِن تَابُوا وَأَقَامُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ** — "Yet if they repent and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then they are your brethren in faith." (9:11).

**His saying, the Exalted:** الشهر الحرام بالشهر الحرام والحرمات قصاص (The sacred month for the sacred month, and all sacred things are [subject to] retaliation). "Sacred things" (الحرمات) is the plural of "sanctity" (حرمة), which is that which is forbidden to violate and mandatory to venerate and respect. The sacred things are: the sanctity of the sacred month, the sanctity of the Sanctuary (الحرم), and the sanctity of the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام). The meaning is that if they were to violate the sanctity of the sacred month by fighting in it—and they did violate it when they barred the Prophet and his companions from the Pilgrimage in the year of al-Hudaybiyyah and pelted them with arrows and stones—it became permissible for the believers to fight them in it, and this is not a violation. For they are only striving in the way of Allah and obeying His command in exalting His word. And if they violated the sanctity of the Sanctuary and the Sacred Mosque by fighting in it or near it, it became permissible for the believers to treat them reciprocally. Thus, His saying: "The sacred month for the sacred month" is a specific explanation followed by a general explanation that includes all sacred things. Even more general than this general explanation is His saying immediately after: "So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you." The meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, only legislated retaliation (القصاص) in the sacred month because He legislated retaliation in all sacred things, and He only legislated retaliation in sacred things because He legislated the permissibility of reciprocal aggression.

Then He urged them to adhere to the path of caution (الاحتياط) in aggression, because it involves the use of severity, might, power, and other forces that invite tyranny (الطغيان) and deviation from the path of moderation, and Allah, the Glorified, does not love the transgressors. They are in greater need of Allah's love, guardianship, and help; so He said: "and be God-conscious, and know that Allah is with the God-conscious (المتقين)."

As for His command to "assail" (الاعتداء) despite the fact that He does not love the transgressors (المعتدين), aggression is blameworthy when it is not in response to aggression. But when it is in response to aggression, it is nothing but rising above the humiliation of lowliness and ascending from the depths of enslavement, injustice, and oppression—much like pride toward the proud, or the public utterance of evil by one who has been wronged.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وأنفقوا في سبيل الله ولا تلقوا بأيديكم إِلى التهلكة (Spend in the way of Allah, and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction). He commanded the spending of wealth to establish fighting in the way of Allah. The discourse in restricting spending here to being "in the way of Allah" is similar to the restriction of fighting at the beginning of the verses to being "in the way of Allah," as previously mentioned. The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying "with your hands" (بأيديكم) is extra for emphasis. The meaning is: "do not cast your hands into destruction," which is a metaphor for the prohibition against nullifying one's strength, capability, and power, as the hand is the manifestation of these. It is also said that the "بـ" denotes causality and the object of "do not cast" is omitted; the meaning being: "do not cast yourselves by your own hands into destruction." Destruction (التهلكة) and ruin (الهلاك) are the same; it is for a human to reach a state where he does not know where he is. It is on the pattern of *taph'ulah* (تَفْعُلَة) with a *damma* on the *'ayn*; there is no other infinitive (مصدر) in the language on this pattern.

The speech is absolute, intended as a prohibition against everything that leads to ruin, whether through excess (إفراط) or deficiency (تفريط). Just as stinginess and withholding wealth during combat leads to the nullification of strength and the loss of power—and therein lies the ruin of the group by the enemy's victory over them—so too does extravagance (التبذير) by spending all one's wealth lead to poverty and destitution, which lead to the degradation of life and the loss of dignity.

Then He, the Glorified, concluded the speech with excellence (الاحسان), saying: "and do good; indeed Allah loves the virtuous (المحسنين)." What is intended by excellence (الاحسان) is not refraining from fighting or showing pity in killing the enemies of the religion and the like. Rather, excellence is performing an act in a beautiful manner: by fighting in the place of fighting, refraining in the place of refraining, severity in the place of severity, and forgiveness in the place of forgiveness. Repelling the wrongdoer with what he deserves is an act of excellence toward humanity by fulfilling its legitimate right, and a defense of the religion that rectifies its affairs. Similarly, refraining from exceeding the limit in fulfilling a legitimate right is another act of excellence. The love of Allah, the Glorified, is the ultimate goal of religion, and it is the duty of every religious person to draw it from his Lord through following. He said: **قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ** — "Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you’" (3:31). These noble verses—which are the verses of fighting—began with the prohibition of transgression and that Allah does not love the transgressors, and concluded with the command for excellence and that Allah loves the virtuous. In this, there are aspects of beauty that are not hidden.

# The Jihad Commanded by the Quran:

The Quran used to command the Muslims to refrain from fighting and to be patient with every injury in the way of Allah, the Glorified, as He said: **قُلْ يَا أَيُّهَا الْكَافِرُونَ لَا أَعْبُدُ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ وَلَا أَنتُمْ عَابِدُونَ مَا أَعْبُدُ** until His saying: **لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ** — "Say, ‘O faithless ones! I do not worship what you worship... To you your religion, and to me my religion.’" (109:1-6). And He said: **وَاصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ** — "and be patient with what they say" (73:10). And He said: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقِتَالُ** — "Have you not regarded those who were told, ‘Keep your hands off [from fighting], and maintain the prayer, and give the zakat’? But when fighting was prescribed for them..." (4:77). It is as if this verse refers to His saying, the Glorified: **وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّن بَعْدِ إِيمَانِكُمْ كُفَّارًا حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم مِّن بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْحَقُّ فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ** — "Many of the People of the Book desire to turn you back into faithless ones after your belief, out of envy from themselves, after the truth has become manifest to them. So excuse and forbear until Allah issues His edict. Indeed Allah has power over all things. And maintain the prayer and give the zakat." (2:109-110).

Then the verses of fighting were revealed. Among them are the verses of fighting specifically against the polytheists of Mecca and those with them, such as His saying: **أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا وإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِن دِيَارِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّا أَن يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ** — "Permission [to fight] has been given to those who are being fought, because they have been wronged. Indeed Allah is capable of helping them—those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah.’" (22:39-40). It is possible that this verse was revealed regarding the defense commanded at Badr and elsewhere. Likewise His saying: **وَقَاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ الدِّينُ كُلُّهُ لِلَّهِ فإِنِ انتَهَوْا فإِن اللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ وإِن تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ مَوْلَاكُمْ نِعْمَ الْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ** — "Fight them until there is no [more] persecution, and religion becomes exclusively for Allah. But if they desist, then Allah is indeed watchful of what they do. But if they turn away, then know that Allah is your guardian—an excellent guardian and an excellent helper!" (8:39-40). Likewise His saying: **وَقَاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ الَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ** — "Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you, but do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not love transgressors." (2:190).

Among them are the verses of fighting against the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). He said: **قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَلَا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ** — "Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, being wretched." (9:29).

Among them are the verses of fighting against the polytheists in general, who are not from the People of the Book, such as His saying: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ** — "then kill the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:5), and His saying: **قَاتِلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ كَافَّةً كَمَا يُقَاتِلُونَكُمْ كَافَّةً** — "and fight the polytheists all together just as they fight you all together" (9:36).

Among them are those that command fighting the disbelievers (الكفار) in general, such as His saying: **قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُم مِّنَ الْكُفَّارِ وَلْيَجِدُوا فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً** — "fight those of the faithless who are in your vicinity, and let them find severity in you" (9:123).

The summary of the matter is that the Quran mentions that Islam and the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) are built upon the foundation of the primordial nature (الفطرة), and it is the guardian (القيم) over the rectification of humanity in its life, as He said: **فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind (there is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know)" (30:30). Thus, establishing it and preserving it is the most important of legitimate human rights, as He said: **شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’" (42:13). Then He mentions that defense (الدفاع) of this legitimate natural right is another natural right. He said: **وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَامِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَاتٌ وَمَسَاجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللَّهِ كَثِيرًا وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ اللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ** — "Were it not for Allah’s repelling some people by means of others, monasteries, churches, synagogues and mosques where the name of Allah is much invoked would have been pulled down. Surely Allah will help those who help Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty." (22:40). Thus, He explained that the standing of the religion of monotheism and the life of its remembrance are contingent upon defense. Similar to this is His saying: **وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ الْأَرْضُ** — "Were it not for Allah’s repelling some people by means of others, the earth would surely have been corrupted" (2:251). He also said within the verses of fighting in Surah al-Anfal: **لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْبَاطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ** — "that He might confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse" (8:8). Then He said after several verses: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ** — "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life" (8:24). He named Jihad (الجهاد) and the fighting to which the believers are summoned as that which gives them life. This means that fighting—whether under the title of defending the Muslims or the core of Islam (بيضة الإسلام), or whether it was initial fighting—is all in reality a defense of the right of humanity to its life. For in polytheism (الشرك) against Allah, the Glorified, lies the destruction of humanity and the death of the primordial nature (الفطرة), and in fighting—which is a defense of its right—lies the restoration of its life and its revival after death.

From there, the intelligent and discerning person senses that Islam ought to have a defensive ruling regarding the cleansing of the earth from the filth of absolute polytheism and the purification of faith for Allah, the Glorified. For this fighting mentioned in the cited verses is either to kill the manifest polytheism of idolatry, or to exalt the word of truth over the word of the People of the Book by compelling them to pay the tribute (الجزية), even though the verse of fighting them includes that they do not believe in Allah and His Messenger nor practice the true religion. Thus, even if they are ostensibly on monotheism, they are polytheists in reality, harboring it within. Defense of the natural human right necessitates bringing them to the true religion.

Although the Quran does not contain an explicit command for this ruling, it openly promises a day for the believers over their enemies, the matter of which will not be completed except by fulfilling the command at this level of fighting—which is fighting to establish sincerity in monotheism (التوحيد). He said: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُشْرِكُونَ** — "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse." (61:9). More manifest than this is His saying: **وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِن بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ** — "Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’" (21:105). And more explicit than that is His saying: **وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضَىٰ لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُم مِّنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْنًا يَعْبُدُونَنِي لَا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئًا** — "Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely replace their fear with security, [for] they worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me." (24:55). His saying "they worship Me" means the worship of sincerity with the reality of faith, by the context of His saying "not ascribing any partners to Me," even though He considers some faith to be polytheism (شرك), as He said: **وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ** — "And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him." (12:106). This is what He promised of purifying the earth and vacating it for the believers on a day when none but Allah is truly worshipped.

One might imagine that this is a promise of divine help through a hidden reformer without recourse to manifest causes. However, this is contradicted by His saying: "He will surely make them successors in the earth," for succession (الاستخلاف) only occurs by the departure of some and their removal from their place and the putting of others in their stead; thus, it contains an allusion to fighting.

Furthermore, His saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ يُجَاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَلَا يَخَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لَائِمٍ** — "O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, dignified before the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer." (5:54)—as will come in its place—points to a true call and a religious uprising that will occur by divine command. This supports the idea that this promised event will occur through a call to Jihad.

From the preceding explanation, the answer appears to what is sometimes raised against Islam regarding its legislation of Jihad: that it is a departure from the mode of religious movements narrated from previous prophets. It is claimed that their religions relied in their course and progress on invitation (الدعوة) and guidance (الهداية) alone, without coercion (الإكراه) into faith through fighting that entails killing, captivity, and raids. For this reason, some—such as Christian missionaries—have called it the "religion of the sword and blood," and others the "religion of compulsion and coercion."

This is because the Quran explains that Islam is built upon the decree of the human primordial nature (الفطرة), which should not be doubted that the perfection of man in his life is what it decrees, judges, and invites toward. It decrees that monotheism (التوحيد) is the foundation upon which individual and social laws must be built, and that defense of this principle by spreading it among people and preserving it from destruction and corruption is a legitimate human right that must be fulfilled by any possible means. Moderation has been observed in this: it began with pure invitation and patience with injury for the sake of Allah, then defense of the core of Islam and the lives, honor, and wealth of Muslims, then initial fighting which is a defense of the right of humanity and the word of monotheism. No fighting was initiated except after the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة) through beautiful invitation, as was the Prophetic Sunnah. He said: **ادْعُ إِلَىٰ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ وَجَادِلْهُم بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ** — "Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good advice and dispute with them in a manner that is best" (16:125). The verse is absolute. He also said: **لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَن بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيَىٰ مَنْ حَيَّ عَن بَيِّنَةٍ** — "so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof, and he who lives might live by a clear proof" (8:42).

As for what they mentioned regarding it necessitating coercion (الإكراه) upon victory, there is no harm in it once the revival of humanity depends on imposing the legitimate truth upon a number of individuals after the explanation and the establishment of the conclusive proof (الحجة البالغة) against them. This is a method prevalent among nations and states; for the rebel who deviates from civil laws is invited to follow them, then they are imposed upon him by any means possible, even if it leads to fighting, until he obeys and submits, willingly or unwillingly.

Furthermore, coercion only lives and lasts in one generation; then religious education and training rectify the coming generations by raising them upon the natural religion and the word of monotheism willingly.

As for their claim that other prophets proceeded only with invitation and guidance, the existing history of their lives indicates that their scope was not wide enough to allow them to engage in fighting, such as Noah, Hud, and Salih (peace be upon them), for they were surrounded by coercion and sovereignty from every side. Similarly was Jesus (peace be upon him) during the days of his stay among the people and his engagement in invitation; his call only spread and his proof was only accepted at the time when abrogation (النسخ) was occurring to his law, which was during the rise of Islam.

Moreover, a group of prophets did fight in the way of Allah, as the Torah relates and the Quran mentions parts of. He said: **وَكَأَيِّن مِّن نَّبِيٍّ قَاتَلَ مَعَهُ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ فَمَا وَهَنُوا لِمَا أَصَابَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَمَا ضَعُفُوا وَمَا اسْتَكَانُوا وَاللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الصَّابِرِينَ وَمَا كَانَ قَوْلَهُمْ إِلَّا أَن قَالُوا رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَإِسْرَافَنَا فِي أَمْرِنَا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَانصُرْنَا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكَافِرِينَ** — "How many a prophet there has been with whom a multitude of godly men fought. They did not falter for what befell them in the way of Allah, neither did they weaken, nor did they abase themselves; and Allah loves the steadfast. All that they said was, ‘Our Lord, forgive us our sins and our excesses in our affairs, make our feet steady, and help us against the faithless lot.’" (3:146-147). He also said—relating Moses' call to his people to fight the giants: **وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ** until He said: **يَا قَوْمِ ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَرْتَدُّوا عَلَىٰ أَدْبَارِكُمْ فَتَنقَلِبُوا خَاسِرِينَ** until He said: **قَالُوا يَا مُوسَىٰ إِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَا أَبَدًا مَّا دَامُوا فِيهَا فَاذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقَاتِلَا إِنَّا هَاهُنَا قَاعِدُونَ** — "And when Moses said... ‘O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you, and do not turn your backs, or you will become losers.’ ... They said, ‘O Moses, we will never enter it so long as they are in it. Go ahead, you and your Lord, and fight! We will be sitting right here.’" (5:20-24). And He said: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى الْمَلَإِ مِن بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ مِن بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰ إِذْ قَالُوا لِنَبِيٍّ لَّهُمُ ابْعَثْ لَنَا مَلِكًا نُّقَاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ** — "Have you not regarded the elite of the Israelites after Moses, when they said to a prophet of theirs, ‘Appoint a king for us, that we may fight in the way of Allah’?" (2:246), until the end of the story of Saul (طالوت) and Goliath (جالوت).

He also said in the story of Solomon and the Queen of Sheba: **أَلَّا تَعْلُوا عَلَيَّ وَأْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ** until He said: **ارْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُم بِجُنُودٍ لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُم بها وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُم مِّنْهَا أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ** — "‘Do not defy me, and come to me in submission.’ ... ‘Go back to them, for we will come to them with hosts they cannot withstand, and we will expel them from it, abased and wretched.’" (27:31-37). This which he threatened them with—"we will come to them with hosts they cannot withstand"—was nothing but initial fighting following an initial invitation.

## (Social Discussion)

There is no doubt that society, wherever it exists—such as the society of the human species and other various specific societies we observe in types of animals—is built upon the foundation of the natural need (الاحتياج الفطري) present within them, which is intended for the preservation of existence and survival.

Just as the primordial nature (الفطرة) and innate disposition (الجبلة) gave them the right of disposal (التصرف) over everything they benefit from in their lives for the preservation of existence and survival—as man disposes of minerals, plants, and animals, and even other humans by any possible means, seeing for himself a right in that even if it interferes with the rights of other animals or the perfection of plants and minerals—and as types of animals do in their disposals of others, acknowledging that they have a right in that; similarly, it gave them the right of defense (الدفاع) of their legitimate rights according to their nature. For the right of disposal is not complete without the right of defense. The world is an abode of interference (تزاحم), and the law is the law of the struggle for survival. Thus, every species that preserves its existence and survival through perception and movement sees for itself a natural right of defense of its rights and acknowledges that this is permissible for it, just as it acknowledges the permissibility of its aforementioned disposal. This is evidenced by what we observe in animal species: they resort during conflict to their bodily tools suitable for use in defense, such as horns, tusks, claws, hooves, thorns, beaks, and the like. Some that are not armed with any of these strong natural weapons resort to flight, hiding, or remaining still, like some prey, turtles, and certain insects. Some that are capable of employing ruses and schemes may take to them in defense, like monkeys, bears, foxes, and their like.

Man, among animals, is armed with intellectual perception (الشعور الفكري) by which he is able to employ others for the sake of defense, just as he is able to do so for the sake of disposal for benefit. He has a nature like other species, and his nature has a judgment and decree. Among its judgments is that man has a right to disposal, and a right to defend his natural right. This right, which man acknowledges by his nature, is what drives him toward fighting and combat in all instances where he is concerned with them in human society, rather than the law of employment (الاستخدام) which he judges as a primary natural judgment—whereby he employs everything he can in the path of his vital interests. For this judgment is moderated by society; when man senses his need to employ others of his kind, and then knows that other individuals of his kind are like him in need, he is forced into reconciliation and agreement upon civilization and social justice—that he serves others to the extent that he employs others, according to what need weighs with its scale and society moderates with its moderation.

From here it is known that man does not rely in any of his fightings upon the law of absolute employment and enslavement which he acknowledges at his first instance. For this is an absolute judgment that man himself abrogated upon his first entry into society, admitting that he should not dispose of the benefits of others except to the extent that he gives others of the benefits of himself. Rather, he only relies in that upon the right of defense of his rights in his benefits; he assumes a right for himself, then observes its loss, so he rises to defend it.

Thus, every fighting is a defense in reality. Even the conquering kings and the prevailing states assume for themselves a kind of right, such as the right of sovereignty and the fitness to command others, or a hardship in living, or a narrowness in land, or the like; they use these as excuses in their attacks on people, the shedding of blood, the corruption of the earth, and the destruction of crops and progeny.

It has been clarified that defense of human rights is a legitimate natural right, the fulfillment of which is permissible for man. However, since this is a right sought for something else and not for itself, it must be balanced against the importance of what belongs to others. One should not proceed with defense unless what man loses by defending is less in vital importance than the lost right being recovered. The Quran has established that the most important of human rights is monotheism (التوحيد) and the religious laws built upon it, just as the rational people of human society agree that the most important of its rights is the right to life under the laws governing human society that preserve the interests of individuals in their lives.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And fight in the way of Allah..." the verse: This verse was revealed regarding the Peace of al-Hudaybiyyah. That was when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم went out with his companions in the year they intended the Umrah—and they were one thousand four hundred. They traveled until they camped at al-Hudaybiyyah, and the polytheists barred them from the Sacred House. They slaughtered the sacrificial animals at al-Hudaybiyyah, then the polytheists made peace with them on the condition that he return that year and come back the following year, and Mecca would be vacated for him for three days to circumambulate the House and do as he wished. He returned to Medina immediately. When the following year came, the Prophet and his companions prepared for the Umrah of Redress (عمرة القضاء). They feared that Quraysh would not fulfill their promise and would bar them from the Sacred House and fight them. The Messenger of Allah disliked fighting them in the sacred month in the Sanctuary, so Allah revealed this verse.

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through various chains from Ibn Abbas and others.

Also in *al-Majma'*, from al-Rabi' ibn Anas and Abd al-Rahman ibn Zayd ibn Aslam: This is the first verse revealed regarding fighting. When it was revealed, the Messenger of Allah would fight those who fought him and refrain from those who refrained from him, until "kill the polytheists wherever you find them" was revealed, so it abrogated this verse.

**I say:** This is an exercise of personal judgment (اجتهاد) on their part. You have already learned that the verse does not abrogate the other; rather, it is a case of generalizing the ruling after its specification.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And kill them wherever you confront them..." the verse: It was revealed concerning a man from the companions who killed a man from the disbelievers in the sacred month. The believers were criticized for that, so Allah, the Glorified, explained that persecution (الفتنة) in religion—which is polytheism—is greater than killing the polytheists in the sacred month, even if it is not permissible.

**I say:** You have already learned that the apparent unity of context in the noble verses indicates they were revealed all at once.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And fight them until there is no more persecution..." the verse: through various chains from Qatadah, who said: "And fight them until there is no more persecution," meaning polytheism, "and religion belongs to Allah." He said: "Until it is said: 'There is no god but Allah.' Upon this the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم fought, and to this he invited." And it was mentioned to us that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم used to say: "Indeed Allah commanded me to fight the people until they say: 'There is no god but Allah.' Then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility except against the wrongdoers." He said: "And the wrongdoer is he who refuses to say 'There is no god but Allah'; he is fought until he says: 'There is no god but Allah.'"

**I say:** His saying "And the wrongdoer..." is from the words of Qatadah; he derived that from the words of the Prophet, and it is a fine derivation. Something similar has been narrated from Ikrimah.

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bukhari, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Umar: Two men came to him during the persecution (فتنة) of Ibn al-Zubayr and said: "The people have done what they have done, and you are the son of Umar and a companion of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, so what prevents you from coming out?" He said: "What prevents me is that Allah has forbidden the blood of my brother." They said: "Did not Allah say: 'And fight them until there is no more persecution'?" He said: "We fought until there was no more persecution and religion belonged to Allah, while you want to fight until there is persecution and religion belongs to other than Allah."

**I say:** He erred in the meaning of persecution (الفتنة), and the two questioners erred as well, as has been explained. Rather, the case is one of the instances of corruption in the earth or fighting out of rebellion (بغي), and it is not permissible for the believers to remain silent regarding it.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And fight them until there is no more persecution..." the verse: He said, meaning polytheism. He said: And it is narrated from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "The sacred month for the sacred month," from al-Ala ibn al-Fudayl, who said: I asked him about the polytheists: do the Muslims initiate fighting against them in the sacred month? He said: If the polytheists initiate it by deeming it lawful, the Muslims may see fit to prevail over them in it. That is His saying: "The sacred month for the sacred month, and all sacred things are subject to retaliation."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, and al-Nahhas in his *al-Nasikh* extracted from Jabir ibn Abdillah, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم would not raid in the sacred month until he was raided; and he would raid, but if it [the month] arrived, he would stay until it passed.

In *al-Kafi*, from Mu'awiyah ibn Ammar, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah about a man who killed a man in the non-sacred area (الحل) then entered the Sanctuary (الحرم). He عليه‌السلام said: "He is not killed, nor fed, nor given drink, nor traded with until he leaves the Sanctuary, then the legal punishment (الحد) is carried out upon him." I said: "What do you say about a man who killed in the Sanctuary or stole?" He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "The legal punishment is carried out upon him in the Sanctuary because he did not respect the sanctity of the Sanctuary. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said: **فَمَنِ اعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ** — 'So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you' (2:194)." He said: "This is in the Sanctuary." He said: "There is no hostility except against the wrongdoers."

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction," he said: "If a man were to spend all that is in his hands in the way of Allah, it would not be good nor would he be successful. Does not Allah say: 'and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction, and do good; indeed Allah loves the virtuous'—meaning the moderate (المقتصدين)!"

Al-Saduq narrated from Thabit ibn Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah said: "Obedience to the ruler is obligatory, and whoever leaves obedience to the ruler has left obedience to Allah and entered into His prohibition. Allah says: 'and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, through many chains from Aslam Abu Imran, who said: We were at Constantinople. Over the people of Egypt was Uqbah ibn Amir, and over the people of Syria was Fadalah ibn Ubayd. A great rank of Romans came out, so we lined up for them. A man from the Muslims charged at the Roman rank until he entered among them. The people shouted and said: "Glory be to Allah! He is casting himself with his own hands into destruction." Abu Ayyub, the companion of the Messenger of Allah, stood up and said: "O people, you interpret this verse with this interpretation, but this verse was only revealed concerning us, the assembly of the Ansar. When Allah had strengthened His religion and its helpers became many, some of us said to others secretly, without the Messenger of Allah: 'Our wealth has been lost, and Allah has strengthened Islam and its helpers have become many. If only we were to stay with our wealth and rectify what was lost of it.' So Allah revealed to His Prophet, refuting what we said: 'And spend in the way of Allah, and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction.' Thus, the destruction was staying with the wealth and rectifying it, and leaving the raiding."

**I say:** The variation in the narrations, as you see, regarding the meaning of the verse supports what we mentioned: that the verse is absolute, including both sides of excess and deficiency in spending together; rather, it encompasses spending and other matters.
